entities.name: GROUNDNUT
entities.description: A major source of financial and food security for Tanzanian smallholder farmers.

entities.name: TANZANIA
entities.description: The country where groundnut production is underdeveloped and the study is conducted.

entities.name: NIGERIA
entities.description: An African nation used for yield comparison with Tanzania.

entities.name: YIELD
entities.description: The amount of groundnut produced per unit area, reported to be lower in Tanzania.

entities.name: CLIMATE VARIABILITY
entities.description: A factor contributing to the lower yields of groundnuts.

entities.name: PESTS AND DISEASES
entities.description: Biological factors contributing to the lower yields of groundnuts.

entities.name: FARMING TECHNIQUES
entities.description: The methods used for cultivation, noted as outdated in the study.

entities.name: TECHNICAL EFFICIENCY
entities.description: A measure of how efficiently inputs are converted into outputs in groundnut production.

entities.name: TECHNOLOGICAL GAP RATIO
entities.description: A ratio used to analyze the gap in technology adoption across regions.

entities.name: META FRONTIER MODEL
entities.description: A two-step statistical model used to analyze technical efficiencies and technological gap ratios.

entities.name: CENTRAL ZONE
entities.description: One of the four main groundnut-producing regions in Tanzania.

entities.name: LAKE ZONE
entities.description: One of the four main groundnut-producing regions in Tanzania.

entities.name: SOUTHERN ZONE
entities.description: One of the four main groundnut-producing regions in Tanzania.

entities.name: SOUTHERN HIGHLAND ZONE
entities.description: One of the four main groundnut-producing regions in Tanzania.

entities.name: META TECHNICAL EFFICIENCY
entities.description: A measure of efficiency relative to the best possible technology across regions.

entities.name: PRODUCTION EFFICIENCY
entities.description: The overall efficiency of groundnut production that needs enhancement to increase productivity.
